text: The surgeon displays the left inguinal hernia, which appears indirect, while the right side shows no visible hernia from the peritoneal surface. The surgical approach involves creating one large flap from right to left across the bilateral inguinal region.

The surgeon performs medial dissection followed by lateral dissection, checking the cord structures. The peritoneum is seen extending into the hernia, while the surgeon sweeps the cord structures down and off, exposing the vas deferens.
The surgeon approaches the end of the peritoneal sac during the dissection, ensuring complete removal. The edge of the sac is visible as it is being extracted.

The surgeon parietalizes the cord, revealing the femoral space and performing an inferior dissection below the obturator space. The cord is further parietalized, exposing anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small lipoma and proceeds to excise it, ensuring its removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon begins addressing the right side, focusing on the renal canal area where clinical exams and CT scans indicated an abnormality, despite the absence of visible intraperitoneal findings.

The surgeon attempts to grasp a structure within the surgical field, initially uncertain if it is part of the cord structures. The decision is made to proceed with a medial approach to detach the peritoneal lining from the underlying tissue.

The surgeon encounters thick and scarred tissue while dissecting, attempting to isolate the area despite uncertainty about its origin. The tissue is carefully manipulated to proceed with the dissection, ensuring no cord structures are involved.
The surgeon attempts to sweep a thickened tubular structure down and off, but encounters difficulty due to the dense, scarred, fibrotic tissue.

The surgeon navigates through fibrous tissue, observing fibers that may resemble mesh, and successfully reaches a normal anatomical plane where the spermatic cord is visible and positioned away from the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully checks the surgical field before transecting, ensuring no inadvertent injury to the cord structures visible in the canal. The cord and vas are identified, and the previously palpable lump in the groin is no longer present.
The surgeon completes the dissection, reducing the obturator fat plug to make room for mesh placement. The unidentified structure is left intact, presumed to be a hydrosil.

The surgeon observes reduced fatty tissue and confirms no injury to surrounding structures. The dissection is complete on both sides, and a sixteen by twelve anatomic pro grip mesh is placed over the dissected area.
The surgeon places the left-sided mesh, ensuring the previously reduced structure is positioned behind the mesh on the peritoneal side, visible on the abdominal side.
The surgeon proceeds with the closure of the peritoneal flap, using sutures to secure the tissue layers.
